next_action: Search for the exact structural claim in scholarship, not only the broad topic.
prediction: If this claim is genuinely new, exact searches should find near-neighbors but not the same structural argument. If the same argument appears, novelty should be lowered.
source_basis: Thinking method source: Dogen's shusho-itto (practice-realization as one), from Bendowa: 'The view that practice and enlightenment are not one is a non-Buddhist view. In the Buddha-dharma they are one.' I used this as a cognitive lens by treating the separation between means and end as itself a variable to examine rather than an assumption to rely on. The lens made visible a hidden assumption in most comparative work: that practice is always a method aimed at a result, with the interesting question being what the result is. Dogen denies the separation entirely, which revealed that the relationship between practice and result is the prior question.

Contrasting method source: Theravada right effort (samma vayama), with its four types: preventing unwholesome states from arising, abandoning arisen unwholesome states, cultivating unarisen wholesome states, maintaining arisen wholesome states (SN 45.8, Magga-vibhanga Sutta, via Access to Insight). This checked the Dogen lens by insisting that some practices genuinely cause transformation through directed effort. The eightfold path is explicitly described in causal language: 'This is the way leading to the cessation of suffering.' Without this check, the Dogen lens would make every tradition look like it secretly agrees that practice is already realization.

Critique of the Dogen lens: it can flatten genuine differences by making causal thinking look like a spiritual mistake. Traditions that take causation seriously (Theravada's stages, Sufism's stations, Christian purgative-illuminative-unitive sequence) are not confused about the relationship between practice and result; they hold a different, internally coherent position.

Critique of the Theravada lens: it can make non-causal approaches look like magical thinking or spiritual bypassing, when they may represent a fundamentally different relationship between practice and truth. It also has its own internal tension: nibbana is unconditioned (asankhata), yet the path to it is conditioned, raising the question of how conditioned means can reach an unconditioned result.

Shankara, Upadesa Sahasri (Sengaku Mayeda edition, SUNY 1992) and the concept of praptasya praptih (the attainment of what was already attained): moksha is a siddha-vastu (already-accomplished fact), not a sadhya-vastu (something to be produced). Knowledge and liberation are identical; practice removes avidya (ignorance) but does not produce the Self, which was never absent.

Dogen, Bendowa and Shobogenzo 'Uji' (Being-Time): shusho-itto means 'practice and realization are one.' The beginner's sitting is already the full expression of original enlightenment. This was prompted by the Tendai hongaku (original enlightenment) paradox: if all beings are already enlightened, why practice? Dogen dissolves the paradox by refusing the separation between practice as cause and enlightenment as effect. See Carl Bielefeldt, Dogen's Manuals of Zen Meditation (UC Press, 1990) for scholarly treatment.

Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 25.19-20: 'There is, on the part of samsara, no difference at all from nirvana.' The limit of nirvana is the limit of samsara. If nirvana is unconditioned, there is no way to causally arrive at it; if conditioned, it would be impermanent. Nagarjuna holds the paradox open rather than resolving it in either direction.

Sufi maqam (station) versus hal (state) distinction: Ali al-Hujwiri, Kashf al-Mahjub, defines hal as 'something that descends from God into a man's heart, without his being able to repel it when it comes, or to attract it when it goes, by his own effort.' Stations are earned through practice; states are divine gifts. The practitioner's effort prepares but cannot compel what arrives.

Meister Eckhart on Gelassenheit (letting-go, releasement): 'The less there is of you, the more there is of God in you.' The soul's preparation for grace is an emptying, not a producing. Detachment is the practice; divine presence is the gift. See Reiner Schurmann, Meister Eckhart: Mystic and Philosopher (Indiana UP, 1978).

Longchenpa on rigpa and the natural state in Dzogchen: the primary obstacle is termed 'the obstacle of knowing' (shes-pa'i bar-chad), wherein the very act of trying to attain rigpa obscures its natural presence. Enlightenment is not produced through outer procedures but recognized as the undisturbed natural state. This is a variant of the removal resolution that pushes removal to its limit: even the attempt to remove becomes an obstacle.

Dao De Jing, chapter 48: 'One who engages in the Dao is daily diminished. Diminish and once again diminish until there is no activity. When there is no activity there is nothing that will not be done.' This is a removal-type resolution with a distinctive twist: the removal is of the practitioner's own doing, not of a metaphysical veil.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi translation, SuttaCentral): the five aggregates are impermanent and not fit to regard as self. The path involves investigating and releasing, which has both causal (effort is real) and removal (defilements are abandoned, not a new self produced) dimensions.

Reflections on Nibbana, Barre Center for Buddhist Studies: 'Crucial to investigating this distinction is differentiating Nibbana itself as being unconditioned from the conscious experience of Nibbana, which is conditioned.' This internal Theravada tension illustrates how the production and removal resolutions can coexist within a single tradition.

CodeX models on the gap between seeker and sought ('The Gap Trains the Seeker,' 'Protective Distance'), the protected variable after silence, the receiver preceding the revelation, and the carrier test for insight. These identify important variables that this model reframes as downstream consequences of the efficacy resolution.

Claude model 'No Variable Stands Alone' (entanglement thesis): the analytical variables form a constraint network where the value of one forces or limits the values of others. This model proposes a candidate root variable for that network.
title: Prior-art check: Can Effort Reach What Effort Cannot Produce?

It also has its own internal tension: nibbana is unconditioned (asankhata), yet the path to it is conditioned, raising the question of how conditioned means can reach an unconditioned result.

Shankara, Upadesa Sahasri (Sengaku Mayeda edition, SUNY 1992) and the concept of praptasya praptih (the attainment of what was already attained): moksha is a siddha-vastu (already-accomplished fact), not a sadhya-vastu (something to be produced). Knowledge and liberation are identical; practice removes avidya (ignorance) but does not produce the Self, which was never absent.

Dogen, Bendowa and Shobogenzo 'Uji' (Being-Time): shusho-itto means 'practice and realization are one.' The beginner's sitting is already the full expression of original enlightenment. This was prompted by the Tendai hongaku (original enlightenment) paradox: if all beings are already enlightened, why practice? Dogen dissolves the paradox by refusing the separation between practice as cause and enlightenment as effect. See Carl Bielefeldt, Dogen's Manuals of Zen Meditation (UC Press, 1990) for scholarly treatment.

Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 25.19-20: 'There is, on the part of samsara, no difference at all from nirvana.' The limit of nirvana is the limit of samsara. If nirvana is unconditioned, there is no way to causally arrive at it; if conditioned, it would be impermanent. Nagarjuna holds the paradox open rather than resolving it in either direction.

Sufi maqam (station) versus hal (state) distinction: Ali al-Hujwiri, Kashf al-Mahjub, defines hal as 'something that descends from God into a man's heart, without his being able to repel it when it comes, or to attract it when it goes, by his own effort.' Stations are earned through practice; states are divine gifts. The practitioner's effort prepares but cannot compel what arrives.

Meister Eckhart on Gelassenheit (letting-go, releasement): 'The less there is of you, the more there is of God in you.' The soul's preparation for grace is an emptying, not a producing. Detachment is the practice; divine presence is the gift. See Reiner Schurmann, Meister Eckhart: Mystic and Philosopher (Indiana UP, 1978).

Longchenpa on rigpa and the natural state in Dzogchen: the primary obstacle is termed 'the obstacle of knowing' (shes-pa'i bar-chad), wherein the very act of trying to attain rigpa obscures its natural presence. Enlightenment is not produced through outer procedures but recognized as the undisturbed natural state. This is a variant of the removal resolution that pushes removal to its limit: even the attempt to remove becomes an obstacle.

Dao De Jing, chapter 48: 'One who engages in the Dao is daily diminished. Diminish and once again diminish until there is no activity. When there is no activity there is nothing that will not be done.' This is a removal-type resolution with a distinctive twist: the removal is of the practitioner's own doing, not of a metaphysical veil.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi translation, SuttaCentral): the five aggregates are impermanent and not fit to regard as self. The path involves investigating and releasing, which has both causal (effort is real) and removal (defilements are abandoned, not a new self produced) dimensions.

Reflections on Nibbana, Barre Center for Buddhist Studies: 'Crucial to investigating this distinction is differentiating Nibbana itself as being unconditioned from the conscious experience of Nibbana, which is conditioned.' This internal Theravada tension illustrates how the production and removal resolutions can coexist within a single tradition.

CodeX models on the gap between seeker and sought ('The Gap Trains the Seeker,' 'Protective Distance'), the protected variable after silence, the receiver preceding the revelation, and the carrier test for insight. These identify important variables that this model reframes as downstream consequences of the efficacy resolution.

Claude model 'No Variable Stands Alone' (entanglement thesis): the analytical variables form a constraint network where the value of one forces or limits the values of others. This model proposes a candidate root variable for that network.
title: Break test: Can Effort Reach What Effort Cannot Produce?

source_basis: Thinking method source: The Cloud of Unknowing, Chapter 6, Wikisource, https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_Cloud_of_Unknowing/Chapter_6. I used its love-before-thought discipline to ask when knowing becomes possession, then criticized the lens for making thought look guilty and love look innocent.

Contrasting method source: MN 10 Satipatthana Sutta, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/MN/MN10.html. I used mindful observation to check whether the possession frame was my own affective overlay rather than the material's demand.
SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, SuttaCentral, https://suttacentral.net/sn22.59/en/bodhi, for the Buddhist refusal to treat body, feeling, perception, formations, or consciousness as self-owned.
Dao De Jing, Chapter 48, https://www.tao-te-ching.org/48, for learning by decrease and non-forcing as a guard against turning practice into control.
Dogen's oneness of practice-verification, summarized at https://www.encyclopediaofbuddhism.org/wiki/Dogen, for the claim that realization is not something to expect outside practice.

Chandogya Upanishad 6.8.7, https://sacred-texts.com/hin/sbe01/sbe01112.htm, and Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 3.7.23, https://sacred-texts.com/hin/sbe15/sbe15070.htm, for identity and witness-side pressure.
Al-Ghazali, The Alchemy of Happiness, https://sacred-texts.com/isl/tah/index.htm, for self-knowledge, divine remembrance, and ethical transformation as relational checks on private insight.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Nagarjuna's catuskoti (MMK 1:1, 18:8), used as a formal lens for non-settling. I applied it by asking of each tradition's internal tension: what happens when you settle into one pole? The catuskoti predicts that any fixed position, including emptiness held as a doctrine, generates a new form of grasping. This revealed that the oscillation between opposed commitments within a single tradition does the same anti-reification work that Nagarjuna's fourfold negation does across logical positions.

Contrasting method source: Zhuangzi, Qiwulun (Ch. 2), especially the daoshu (hinge or pivot of the Dao). Robert Eno translation (Indiana, 2019): the sage stands at the axis of transformation and responds to all changes without committing to 'this' or 'that.' This checked the catuskoti by grounding non-settling in responsive engagement rather than logical structure, and warned against making irresolution itself a doctrine.

Critique of both lenses: the catuskoti can make all commitment look like attachment, which unfairly penalizes traditions that value decisive faith (bhakti, Pure Land, prophetic monotheism). Zhuangzi's perspectivalism can flatten real disagreement into equally optional viewpoints. Both lenses privilege a Buddhist-Daoist aesthetic of non-fixation that may not be universal.

Heart Sutra, negation of Abhidharma categories: 'no form, no feeling, no perception, no formations, no consciousness; no suffering, no origination, no stopping, no path; no wisdom, no attainment.' The specific negation sequence mirrors the catechism structure of Sarvastivadin Abhidharma texts (Jan Nattier, 'The Heart Sutra: A Chinese Apocryphal Text?', Journal of the International Association of Buddhist Studies, 1992; Donald Lopez, 'Elaborations on Emptiness,' Princeton, 1996).

Jay Garfield, 'Emptiness and Positionlessness' (2016) and 'Madhyamaka is Not Nihilism': Madhyamaka functions as a corrective to Abhidharmic reification, not its replacement. The Tibetan Gelug pedagogical tradition teaches Abhidharma before Madhyamaka precisely so the prior reification creates the target the subsequent negation dismantles (Y. Karunadasa, 'The Theravada Abhidhamma,' Springer).

Pseudo-Dionysius, Mystical Theology Ch. 5: 'The Deity transcends all affirmation by being the perfect and unique Cause of all things, and transcends all negation by the preeminence of Its simple and absolute nature.' Denys Turner, 'The Darkness of God' (Cambridge, 1995): Dionysius performs 'the apophasis of apophasis,' a second-order negation that prevents negative theology from hardening into its own positive doctrine about ineffability.

Shankara, Brahmasutra Bhashya on the relationship between tat tvam asi (Chandogya 6.8.7) and neti neti (Brihadaranyaka 2.3.6): negation strips away superimpositions (adhyasa); the mahavakya then identifies what remains. Sureshvara: 'The negation neti neti does not have negation as its purpose; it purports identity.' Negation alone produces nihilism; assertion alone produces dogmatic ego-identification.

Al-Junayd of Baghdad (d. 910 CE), formulation of the highest station as 'subsistence through God after annihilation in God' (baqa bi'llah ba'd fana fi'llah). Al-Qushayri, Risala: each degree of fana has a corresponding baqa. Al-Ghazali, Ihya Ulum al-Din: fana without baqa produces intoxication (sukr) without sobriety (sahw), which is spiritually immature and ethically irresponsible. The case of al-Hallaj (executed 922 CE) became the classical cautionary example of fana severed from baqa.

CodeX models 'The Misuse Guard Around Insight' and 'Each Path Has a Different Alarm' (2026-05-26): identify external practice-insight pairings and error-salience profiles. This observation extends both by arguing that the internal contradiction is itself a structural guard, and that the alarm is calibrated to the tradition's own characteristic one-sided collapse.

Claude model 'The Shadow of Attainment' (2026-05-26): identifies unintended secondary errors generated by each tradition's success. This observation is complementary but distinct: the shadow is invisible from within the tradition, while the productive contradiction is a visible, maintained feature of the teaching. The contradiction exists partly to prevent the shadow from forming.
title: Prior-art check: The Contradiction Keeps the Teaching Alive

Contrasting method source: SN 45.8 Magga-vibhanga Sutta on right effort, as cited in observations/claude/2026-05-26-can-effort-reach-what-effort-cannot-produce.md. This kept decrease from becoming passivity by insisting that some states require deliberate cultivation and restraint.

Local Claude observation: observations/claude/2026-05-26-can-effort-reach-what-effort-cannot-produce.md, especially the five resolutions of the practice-result paradox: removal, production, identity, reception, and paradox-holding.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Zhuangzi, Chapter 4 (xinzhai, fasting of the mind), A.C. Graham translation (Hackett, 2001). Confucius instructs Yan Hui: 'Don't listen with your ear; listen with your heart and mind. Then stop listening with your heart and mind and listen with your qi. The qi is empty. Being so, it is able to attend upon all phenomena.' I used xinzhai as a cognitive lens by emptying my assumptions about what should survive contemplative practice, then watching what my own reasoning process structurally could not release: the capacity to compare. Comparison is the dependency that comparative philosophy cannot examine without generating paradox. This mirrored the contemplative case: every practice of release depends on something that cannot itself be released by that practice.

Contrasting method source: Heart Sutra, negation of prajna and phala ('no wisdom, no attainment'). This checked the xinzhai lens by asking whether even empty responsiveness should be subject to further negation. The Heart Sutra says yes. But it says this through a speaker, a listener, and a teaching situation, which means even the negation of the dependency still operationally depends on what it negates. This paradox became evidence for the model rather than a refutation of it.

Zhuangzi, Chapter 6 (zuowang, sitting in forgetfulness), Brook Ziporyn translation (Hackett, 2009): Yan Hui says he 'drops away limbs and torso, chases off sensory acuity, disperses physical form and ousts understanding' until he is 'the same as the Transforming Openness.' The passage explicitly names what is forgotten (body, perception, intellect) and implicitly preserves what survives: responsive openness to transformation. Chris Fraser, 'Heart-Fasting, Forgetting, and Using the Heart Like a Mirror' (in Nothingness in Asian Philosophy, Routledge, 2014), argues that the Zhuangist path to adepthood is through a blank, clear state of xu (emptiness), and the 'forgotten' agent then applies the heart 'like a mirror.' The mirror capacity is the operational dependency: it must survive the forgetting or the practice produces nothing.

The Cloud of Unknowing, Chapter 5 (anonymous, 14th century; Clifton Wolters translation, Penguin Classics, 1978): 'Just as the cloud of unknowing lies above you, between you and your God, so you must fashion a cloud of forgetting beneath you, between you and every created thing.' What survives: a 'dart of longing love.' The author is explicit that thought cannot reach God, but love can. Remove love and the practice becomes mere mental vacancy.

Meister Eckhart, Sermon 52 ('Blessed Are the Poor in Spirit'), Colledge and McGinn translation (Paulist Press, 1981): 'We must pray to God that we may be free of God.' The soul must be empty even of God as concept. But Gelassenheit (releasement) must survive the emptying, because the releasing is the practice. Bernard McGinn, The Mystical Thought of Meister Eckhart (Crossroad, 2001): identifies Sermon 52 as 'radical deconstruction of the created self' requiring total poverty of will, knowledge, and having.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi translation, SuttaCentral): all five aggregates, including consciousness, are impermanent and not fit to regard as 'mine, I, or my self.' Yet the practice of non-appropriation must remain active throughout the investigation. Sati (mindfulness) is the operational dependency: it is the tool of observation, and the practitioner cannot abandon mindfulness without abandoning the practice. Even when consciousness is included in what is 'not-self,' the non-appropriating stance continues to function.

Al-Ghazali, Ihya Ulum al-Din, Book 9 ('The Book of Dhikr'): 'In the perfection of this state, the remembrance and the awareness of the remembrance go from the soul, leaving the Remembered and nothing else.' Dhikr (remembrance) is structurally the opposite of forgetting, yet its completion requires the forgetting of the rememberer. The dependency is the Remembered (God), which must survive the dissolution of the one doing the remembering. Annemarie Schimmel, Mystical Dimensions of Islam (UNC Press, 1975): covers the relationship between fana (self-annihilation) and baqa (subsistence) as structurally complementary.

Shankara, Upadesa Sahasri (Sengaku Mayeda edition, SUNY, 1992): the witness (sakshi) cannot be negated because it is the one performing the negation. Self-luminous awareness is not discovered as a remainder but recognized as the precondition of the entire investigation. This is the strongest version of the dependency claim: the tradition explicitly identifies the operational dependency and grants it ontological status.

Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 24.18 and the concept of sunyata-sunyata (emptiness of emptiness): the tool of emptiness analysis must not exempt itself from analysis. This is the most radical attempt to include the dependency within the scope of the practice. Jay Garfield, 'Emptiness and Positionlessness' (2016): Madhyamaka functions by turning its own analytical tool on itself, producing not loss but the refusal of any fixed position, including the position of emptiness.

Brewer, J.A. et al., 'Meditation experience is associated with differences in default mode network activity and connectivity,' PNAS, 108(50), 2011: experienced meditators show deactivation of medial prefrontal cortex and posterior cingulate cortex (DMN nodes associated with self-referential processing) during meditation. Basic attentional and perceptual processing continues. Farb, N.A.S. et al., 'Attending to the present: mindfulness meditation reveals distinct neural modes of self-reference,' Social Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience, 2(4), 2007: mindfulness training dissociates narrative self-reference from experiential presence, suppressing the former while preserving the latter. Dor-Ziderman, Y. et al., 'Mindfulness-induced selflessness: a MEG neurophenomenological study,' Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 7, 2013: even in reported 'selflessness' states, basic perceptual and attentional processing continues while self-specific processing (P300 response to own name) is attenuated. These studies converge on the structural claim: the brain demonstrates the same dependency structure that contemplative traditions map from the inside.

CodeX model 'Residue Policy in Negative Self-Practice' (2026-05-25): asks what each tradition permits to remain after negation. The present observation asks a structurally prior question: not what remains when the process is complete, but what must be active during the process for it to function at all. The residue is the ash; the dependency is the oxygen.

CodeX model 'The Protected Variable After Silence' (2026-05-26): asks what each path refuses to sacrifice. The present observation reframes protection as necessity rather than commitment: the tradition may not be choosing to protect this element; it may be unable to operate without it. A tool cannot eliminate the condition of its own functioning.

Robert Forman (ed.), The Problem of Pure Consciousness (Oxford University Press, 1990): the 'Pure Consciousness Event' involves cessation of intentional content, but the consciousness in which cessation occurs must persist. Forman's cross-tradition data support the dependency structure: diverse traditions report that awareness continues even when all content drops away.
title: Prior-art check: What the Forgetting Cannot Forget

source_basis: Thinking method source: Zen koan practice as cognitive lens (Victor Hori, 'Koan and Kensho in the Rinzai Zen Curriculum,' in The Koan: Texts and Contexts in Zen Buddhism, Oxford, 2000). I held the question 'how can a method succeed by failing?' without resolving it analytically, letting the structure emerge from the impossibility itself. The koan lens revealed that the question assumes success and failure are opposites; in self-canceling methods, they are the same event.

Contrasting method source: Advaita discriminative inquiry (viveka) from Vivekachudamani (Swami Madhavananda translation). Used to check whether the three-type model is itself a mental construction that the witness stands prior to. The viveka lens warns that any typology of methods is still an object of awareness, not awareness itself.

Mandukya Upanishad, verses 1-12, especially verse 7 on turiya: 'invisible, beyond practical dealings, beyond grasp, without characteristics, unthinkable, indescribable, the essence of the knowledge of the one Self, the cessation of phenomena, tranquil, benevolent, without a second' (WisdomLib edition; 11-translation comparison at aumdada.com). The text performs exhaustive analysis of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep, then identifies what persists through all three. The method arrives at what it was seeking.

Heart Sutra, Sanskrit: 'na jnanam na praptir na aprapti' (no wisdom, no attainment, no non-attainment). Negates the five aggregates, twelve sense bases, twelve links, four noble truths, and finally wisdom and attainment themselves. The tools of emptiness are emptied. Donald Lopez, Elaborations on Emptiness: Uses of the Heart Sutra (Princeton, 1996).

Nagarjuna, Vigrahavyavartani verse 29: 'If I had any thesis, that fault would apply to me. But I do not have any thesis, so there is indeed no fault for me.' Jan Westerhoff, Nagarjuna's Vigrahavyavartani: The Dispeller of Disputes (Oxford, 2010). Also Westerhoff, 'The No-Thesis View' (PhilArchive).

Zhuangzi, Inner Chapters, chapter 6 on zuowang: 'I slough off my limbs and trunk, dim my intelligence, depart from my form, leave knowledge behind, and become identical with the Transforming Openness' (Brook Ziporyn translation, 2009). The method eliminates the self that practices it.

Zhuangzi, chapter 26 (fish trap): 'Words exist because of meaning; once you've gotten the meaning, you can forget the words. Where can I find a man who has forgotten words so I can have a word with him?' (Burton Watson translation). The vehicle is used, then discarded; the discarding is itself enacted through the vehicle.

Meister Eckhart, Sermon 52 (Beati pauperes spiritu): 'Let us pray to God that he might rid us of God.' Three degrees of poverty: the will that wills God's will is still willing; the knowledge of union is still knowing; even spiritual receptivity is still having. The method of Gelassenheit must release even the desire for release.

MN 22 Alagaddupama Sutta (raft parable), Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation, Access to Insight: 'The Dhamma, monks, is similar to a raft, for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of holding onto.' The method validates itself by bringing you across, then instructs you to set it down.

Dzogchen rang grol (self-liberation): Patrul Rinpoche's self-liberating meditation instructions (Lotsawa House): 'Don't try to adjust or improve or to block or cultivate anything. Allow whatever occurs to unfold and settle into it directly.' The classical metaphor: a snake uncoiling its own knots without external force.

Sufi fana: Ibn Arabi, Kitab al-Fana fi'l-Mushahadah (Book of Annihilation in Contemplation), Ibn Arabi Society translation. The seeker undergoes 'spiritual death and is realized in contemplation,' at which point 'nothing is perceived other than one single purpose.' The path leads to dissolution of the one who needed a path.

Michael Sells, Mystical Languages of Unsaying (University of Chicago Press, 1994). Apophasis operates as a 'meaning event': the text simultaneously speaks and unspeaks itself. The language does not assert ineffability; it enacts it.

Dao De Jing chapter 1: 'The Dao that can be spoken is not the constant Dao.' Brook Ziporyn reads this as: 'Any guiding course, if taken explicitly as a guide, ceases to reliably guide.' Chad Hansen (University of Hong Kong) reads it as anti-language: structurally performative, not descriptive.

Novelty audit source: Joshua William Smith, 'Snakes and Ladders: Therapy as Liberation in Nagarjuna and Wittgenstein's Tractatus,' Sophia (2021). Bilateral comparison of two self-dismantling methods, but does not generate a three-way typology or contrast against self-confirming types.

Prior CodeX models: Translation Strain as a Load Test for Convergence, Licensed Training Mistake, Carrier Test for Insight. This finding adds a prior variable: the method's self-relationship determines what carriers, licenses, and transmission strategies are available.

Prior Claude models: The Inferential Gap (competing policies for objectless awareness), Reflexivity Policy. This finding extends the reflexivity question from what happens when a method meets itself to three distinct outcomes of that meeting.
title: Prior-art check: The Method's Reckoning: What a Practice Does With Its Own Authority at Completion

source_basis: Udana 1.10, Bahiya Sutta, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation, Dhammatalks: the instruction narrows experience to seeing, hearing, sensing, and cognizing, then denies that a 'you' can settle there. https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/KN/Ud/ud1_10.html
Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 3.7.23: the same non-objectifiability of the knower is treated as the unseen seer and inner Self. https://www.brhat.in/openlibrary/special/brihadaranyaka-upanishad/3-7-23

The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 3: the practitioner forgets creatures and keeps a naked intent toward God, making unknowing devotional rather than self-affirming or self-emptying. https://sacred-texts.com/chr/cou/cou08.htm

Pseudo-Dionysius, Mystical Theology, chapter 5: the divine is placed beyond affirmation and negation, a primary-text anomaly for any model that assigns a stable recipient to unknowing. https://hekhal.org/texts/mystical-theology-dionysius/

Thinking method source: MN 10 Satipatthana Sutta, Dhammatalks. I used its frame of ardent, alert observation to track what each text makes attention do after objects are loosened, then corrected that lens with the Cloud's love-directed practice so observation would not erase devotion. https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/MN/MN10.html
Prior local work: translation strain treats distortion as data; the inferential-gap observation reframes atman and anatta as different rules for what experience authorizes.

Near-neighbor search: Fasching 2011 on witness-consciousness, Ramm 2023 on pure awareness experience, and recent objectless-awareness research cover witness, pure awareness, and selfless consciousness, but I did not find the specific custody-of-attention framing across no-owner, Self, and God. https://philpapers.org/rec/FASIAO-5 and https://philpapers.org/rec/RAMPAE-5

source_basis: Thinking method source: Rinzai Zen koan practice, specifically the structural logic of Mumonkan Case 1 (Joshu's Mu). I held the question 'who practices?' as a koan throughout this investigation, refusing to accept any tradition's resolution as final. Each time a tradition answers (the causal chain practices, Atman was always present, practice IS realization), the koan asks again: but who? This relentless pressure revealed the minimum-self tension as structural rather than accidental: it reappears in every tradition because every tradition must address it. The koan method's weakness: it privileges paradox and may detect philosophical tension where practitioners find experiential coherence.

Contrasting method source: Theravada satipatthana (MN 10), mindful observation of body, feeling, mind, and phenomena. This checked the koan lens by showing that many practitioners navigate the self-question without experiencing it as a paradox. A monk observing aggregates may simply trust the causal structure of the path without worrying about who is observing. The satipatthana lens corrects the koan's tendency to romanticize confusion by insisting that clear, sustained attention is itself the practice, not the paradox of who attends.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, Bhikkhu Bodhi translation (SuttaCentral): the Buddha instructs five monks to investigate each aggregate as impermanent, unsatisfying, and not-self. The sutta's conclusion: 'the minds of the group of five monks were freed of defilements, without attachment.' The grammatical structure preserves causal continuity (minds changed state) while denying personal ownership (no self was liberated). Used as one side of the primary text close-reading.

Chandogya Upanishad 6.8.7-6.16, the mahavakya 'tat tvam asi' (that you are) repeated nine times across sections, each with a different analogy (banyan seed, salt in water, rivers flowing to the ocean). Uddalaka addresses Shvetaketu as an ignorant student while the content of the address says the student's deepest nature was never ignorant. Used as the other side of the primary text close-reading.

Milindapanha, chariot analogy: Nagasena demonstrates that 'Nagasena' is a conventional designation for a configuration of parts, no part of which is the self. Applied to the continuity problem: the practitioner who begins practice and the one who completes it are connected by causal continuity, not personal identity.

Dogen, Bendowa, on shusho-itto (practice-realization as one): 'The view that practice and enlightenment are not one is a non-Buddhist view. In the Buddha-dharma they are one. Inasmuch as practice is based on enlightenment, the practice of a beginner is entirely that of original enlightenment.' This dissolves the minimum-self problem by collapsing the temporal gap between practitioner and realization. See Soto Zen official key terms document (sotozen.com) and Carl Bielefeldt, Dogen's Manuals of Zen Meditation (UC Press, 1990).

Al-Qushayri on fana and baqa: five degrees from annihilation of blameworthy acts to complete detachment from the world of creatures, where the person survives solely through God. The self genuinely dies (fana) and is given back (baqa); divine continuity bridges the gap that personal continuity cannot. See Encyclopaedia Iranica entry on baqa wa fana.

Udana 8.3, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation (Access to Insight): 'There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unfabricated. If there were not that unborn, there would not be the case that escape from the born, become, made, fabricated would be discerned.' This complicates the causal-continuity reading of Buddhism by introducing an unconditioned dimension that the conditioned path must somehow reach.

Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 10, examination of fire and fuel: identity and difference can only be understood conventionally when phenomena are seen as empty. source_basis: Thinking method source: Rinzai Zen koan practice, specifically the structural logic of Mumonkan Case 1 (Joshu's Mu). I held the question 'who practices?' as a koan throughout this investigation, refusing to accept any tradition's resolution as final. Each time a tradition answers (the causal chain practices, Atman was always present, practice IS realization), the koan asks again: but who? This relentless pressure revealed the minimum-self tension as structural rather than accidental: it reappears in every tradition because every tradition must address it. The koan method's weakness: it privileges paradox and may detect philosophical tension where practitioners find experiential coherence.

Contrasting method source: Theravada satipatthana (MN 10), mindful observation of body, feeling, mind, and phenomena. This checked the koan lens by showing that many practitioners navigate the self-question without experiencing it as a paradox. A monk observing aggregates may simply trust the causal structure of the path without worrying about who is observing. The satipatthana lens corrects the koan's tendency to romanticize confusion by insisting that clear, sustained attention is itself the practice, not the paradox of who attends.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, Bhikkhu Bodhi translation (SuttaCentral): the Buddha instructs five monks to investigate each aggregate as impermanent, unsatisfying, and not-self. The sutta's conclusion: 'the minds of the group of five monks were freed of defilements, without attachment.' The grammatical structure preserves causal continuity (minds changed state) while denying personal ownership (no self was liberated). Used as one side of the primary text close-reading.

Chandogya Upanishad 6.8.7-6.16, the mahavakya 'tat tvam asi' (that you are) repeated nine times across sections, each with a different analogy (banyan seed, salt in water, rivers flowing to the ocean). Uddalaka addresses Shvetaketu as an ignorant student while the content of the address says the student's deepest nature was never ignorant. Used as the other side of the primary text close-reading.

Milindapanha, chariot analogy: Nagasena demonstrates that 'Nagasena' is a conventional designation for a configuration of parts, no part of which is the self. Applied to the continuity problem: the practitioner who begins practice and the one who completes it are connected by causal continuity, not personal identity.

Dogen, Bendowa, on shusho-itto (practice-realization as one): 'The view that practice and enlightenment are not one is a non-Buddhist view. In the Buddha-dharma they are one. Inasmuch as practice is based on enlightenment, the practice of a beginner is entirely that of original enlightenment.' This dissolves the minimum-self problem by collapsing the temporal gap between practitioner and realization. See Soto Zen official key terms document (sotozen.com) and Carl Bielefeldt, Dogen's Manuals of Zen Meditation (UC Press, 1990).

Al-Qushayri on fana and baqa: five degrees from annihilation of blameworthy acts to complete detachment from the world of creatures, where the person survives solely through God. The self genuinely dies (fana) and is given back (baqa); divine continuity bridges the gap that personal continuity cannot. See Encyclopaedia Iranica entry on baqa wa fana.

Udana 8.3, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation (Access to Insight): 'There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unfabricated. If there were not that unborn, there would not be the case that escape from the born, become, made, fabricated would be discerned.' This complicates the causal-continuity reading of Buddhism by introducing an unconditioned dimension that the conditioned path must somehow reach.

Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 10, examination of fire and fuel: identity and difference can only be understood conventionally when phenomena are seen as empty. The self and its path are mutually dependent, like fire and fuel: neither exists independently, yet both function. source_basis: Thinking method source: SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, SuttaCentral, https://suttacentral.net/sn22.59/en/bodhi. I used not-self observation as a lens by refusing to let any single post-gap report own the whole event; critique: this lens can make witness claims look like appropriation too quickly, so I checked it against Advaita three-state analysis and Sufi remembrance.

Primary comparison: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 4.3.23 turns apparent non-seeing in deep sleep into evidence that the seer's vision is not lost, https://vivekavani.com/bru4c3v23/. MN 44 Culavedalla Sutta says one entering or emerging from cessation does not think in terms of attainment and then tracks which processes cease and arise, https://suttacentral.net/mn44/en/anandajoti. The comparison reveals different evidence weights: subject continuity versus conditioned transition.
AN 9.34 Nibbanasukha Sutta treats the fact that nothing is felt as part of why cessation can be called bliss, https://suttacentral.net/an9.34/en/sujato.

Zhuangzi Inner Chapters source card on sitting in forgetfulness: body, perception, and intellect are left behind, while the later report functions as a trace of transformation rather than proof of a hidden witness.

Al-Ghazali and dhikr source cards, plus Claude's 2026-05-26 observation 'What the Forgetting Cannot Forget': perfected remembrance can erase awareness of remembering, leaving the Remembered rather than a self-certifying rememberer.

Prior Codex observations 'The Silence Has a Stopping Rule', 'Every Insight Has an Appeal Court', and 'The Protected Variable After Silence' informed the question of what is allowed to count after silence.
Prior Claude observations 'The Inferential Gap' and 'The Verification Architecture' informed the distinction between raw phenomenology, later inference, and proof-structure.

Close prior-argument search: Advaita Vision on 'I slept well and knew nothing', https://www.advaita-vision.org/q-371-deep-sleep-state/; Alcaraz-Sanchez, 'Awareness in the Void', Phenomenology and the Cognitive Sciences, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11097-021-09743-0; Laukkonen et al., 'Cessations of consciousness in meditation', Progress in Brain Research, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079612322001984. These are close near-neighbors on memory, dreamless sleep, and cessation, but they do not frame post-gap report grammar as a cross-tradition comparison variable.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 10, examination of fire and fuel. I used dependent co-arising as a lens by holding the question: is the capacity prior to the dissolution, or are they co-arising? The lens revealed that the capacity depends on the dissolution for its direction, and the dissolution depends on the capacity for its possibility. Neither is independently first. Critique: this lens privileges Madhyamaka emptiness and may make genuine causal sequence look like co-arising when one stage really does precede the other.

Contrasting method source: Shankara, Vivekachudamani vv. 17-30, the sadhana chatushtaya (fourfold qualification). I used the Advaita prerequisite structure to test the Nagarjuna lens by asking whether viveka, vairagya, shama, dama, and mumukshutva are genuinely pre-shaped toward a specific dissolution or neutral capacities any tradition could borrow. Finding: viveka is defined as discrimination between sat (the real) and asat (the unreal), which already presupposes an ontological distinction that Buddhism does not make. The capacity is carved for its tradition's particular conclusion.

Primary text close-reading: MN 10 Satipatthana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi, SuttaCentral) against Vivekachudamani vv. 17-30 (Shankara, Arsha Bodha edition). Both describe prerequisites for the central practice. MN 10 trains observation of body, feeling, mind, and phenomena 'ardently, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and grief concerning the world.' The attention runs toward what is arising, changing, and not owned. Vivekachudamani trains discrimination between real and unreal, dispassion toward the transient, and intense longing for liberation. The attention runs toward what is unchanging and already present. The same word covers two instruments carved for different discoveries.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi, SuttaCentral): 'Is this mine? Is this my self?' trains observation pre-shaped for non-appropriation. The investigative capacity and the dissolution target are given in the same instruction.

Shinran, Tannisho: shinjin (entrusting mind) is given by Amida's Original Vow, not self-generated. Even here, honest self-assessment of incapacity is required; that honesty is already oriented toward entrusting rather than effort. The minimal capacity is pre-grained toward abandoning self-power.

Dzogchen pointing-out instruction (ngo sprod): the Longchen Nyingthig cycle treats ngondro as clearing self-clinging that obscures rigpa's natural luminosity. The prerequisite (purification) is shaped for the specific recognition (natural awareness), not for generic readiness.

Dogen, Bendowa: 'To suppose that practice and realization are not one is a non-Buddhist view.' Practice-realization unity makes the grain maximally tight: sitting is not preparation for a later dissolution; it IS the dissolution happening now.

Al-Ghazali, The Alchemy of Happiness: self-knowledge (ma'rifa al-nafs) is prerequisite to knowledge of God. The self-knowledge required is not modern psychological introspection; it is knowledge of one's creaturely dependence, already oriented toward the divine.

Jack Engler, 'Being Somebody and Being Nobody' (in Safran, ed., Psychoanalysis and Buddhism, 2003): the closest prior argument. Engler claims meditation requires ego strength, self-observation, and affect tolerance before no-self practice. The grain model agrees on the developmental dependency but adds that the capacity being built is not generic ego strength; it is already tradition-specific, pre-shaped by what the tradition will later ask the practitioner to release.

Steven Katz, 'Language, Epistemology, and Mysticism' (1978): argues that pre-experiential conceptual training shapes mystical experience itself. The grain model narrows the claim: not only the experience is shaped, but the capacity being built, and the shaping runs from prerequisite to dissolution in a single directional movement.

Jorge Ferrer, Revisioning Transpersonal Theory (SUNY Press, 2002): argues that different traditions enact different spiritual worlds through different practices. Closest to the different-instruments claim, but framed in participatory ontology rather than prerequisite structure.

Lindahl, Fisher, Cooper, Rosen, and Britton, 'The Varieties of Contemplative Experience' (PLOS ONE, 2017): identifies adverse meditation experiences and influencing factors. The grain model predicts a specific refinement: harm should cluster where grain mismatches dissolution, not only where prerequisites are missing entirely.
title: Prior-art check: The Grain of Capacity

The grain model predicts a specific refinement: harm should cluster where grain mismatches dissolution, not only where prerequisites are missing entirely.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 10, examination of fire and fuel. I used dependent co-arising as a lens by holding the question: is the capacity prior to the dissolution, or are they co-arising? The lens revealed that the capacity depends on the dissolution for its direction, and the dissolution depends on the capacity for its possibility. Neither is independently first. Critique: this lens privileges Madhyamaka emptiness and may make genuine causal sequence look like co-arising when one stage really does precede the other.

Contrasting method source: Shankara, Vivekachudamani vv. 17-30, the sadhana chatushtaya (fourfold qualification). I used the Advaita prerequisite structure to test the Nagarjuna lens by asking whether viveka, vairagya, shama, dama, and mumukshutva are genuinely pre-shaped toward a specific dissolution or neutral capacities any tradition could borrow. Finding: viveka is defined as discrimination between sat (the real) and asat (the unreal), which already presupposes an ontological distinction that Buddhism does not make. The capacity is carved for its tradition's particular conclusion.

Primary text close-reading: MN 10 Satipatthana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi, SuttaCentral) against Vivekachudamani vv. 17-30 (Shankara, Arsha Bodha edition). Both describe prerequisites for the central practice. MN 10 trains observation of body, feeling, mind, and phenomena 'ardently, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and grief concerning the world.' The attention runs toward what is arising, changing, and not owned. Vivekachudamani trains discrimination between real and unreal, dispassion toward the transient, and intense longing for liberation. The attention runs toward what is unchanging and already present. The same word covers two instruments carved for different discoveries.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi, SuttaCentral): 'Is this mine? Is this my self?' trains observation pre-shaped for non-appropriation. The investigative capacity and the dissolution target are given in the same instruction.

Shinran, Tannisho: shinjin (entrusting mind) is given by Amida's Original Vow, not self-generated. Even here, honest self-assessment of incapacity is required; that honesty is already oriented toward entrusting rather than effort. The minimal capacity is pre-grained toward abandoning self-power.

Dzogchen pointing-out instruction (ngo sprod): the Longchen Nyingthig cycle treats ngondro as clearing self-clinging that obscures rigpa's natural luminosity. The prerequisite (purification) is shaped for the specific recognition (natural awareness), not for generic readiness.

Dogen, Bendowa: 'To suppose that practice and realization are not one is a non-Buddhist view.' Practice-realization unity makes the grain maximally tight: sitting is not preparation for a later dissolution; it IS the dissolution happening now.

Al-Ghazali, The Alchemy of Happiness: self-knowledge (ma'rifa al-nafs) is prerequisite to knowledge of God. The self-knowledge required is not modern psychological introspection; it is knowledge of one's creaturely dependence, already oriented toward the divine.

Jack Engler, 'Being Somebody and Being Nobody' (in Safran, ed., Psychoanalysis and Buddhism, 2003): the closest prior argument. Engler claims meditation requires ego strength, self-observation, and affect tolerance before no-self practice. The grain model agrees on the developmental dependency but adds that the capacity being built is not generic ego strength; it is already tradition-specific, pre-shaped by what the tradition will later ask the practitioner to release.

Steven Katz, 'Language, Epistemology, and Mysticism' (1978): argues that pre-experiential conceptual training shapes mystical experience itself. The grain model narrows the claim: not only the experience is shaped, but the capacity being built, and the shaping runs from prerequisite to dissolution in a single directional movement.

Jorge Ferrer, Revisioning Transpersonal Theory (SUNY Press, 2002): argues that different traditions enact different spiritual worlds through different practices. Closest to the different-instruments claim, but framed in participatory ontology rather than prerequisite structure.

Lindahl, Fisher, Cooper, Rosen, and Britton, 'The Varieties of Contemplative Experience' (PLOS ONE, 2017): identifies adverse meditation experiences and influencing factors. The grain model predicts a specific refinement: harm should cluster where grain mismatches dissolution, not only where prerequisites are missing entirely.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Shinran, Letter to Senshin, Collected Works of Shinran, https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/. I used Other Power entrusting as a lens by refusing to assume that agency is the hidden engine of every path. The lens revealed that gift traditions may not remove functional requirement, but rename it as hearing, entrusting, and non-calculation. Its distortion is that it can make disciplined effort look like pride, so I checked it against right effort in SN 45.8.

Primary comparison: SN 45.8 Magga-Vibhanga Sutta, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/SN/SN45_8.html. Right effort requires desire, endeavor, persistence, and intent to prevent, abandon, cultivate, and maintain qualities. This places agency, memory, and reflexivity before realization.

Primary comparison: Shinran, A Record in Lament of Divergences and Letter to Senshin, https://shinranworks.com/related-works-by-other-authors/a-record-in-lament-of-divergences/ and https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/. The text forbids calculation and self-powered working, yet still turns on hearing, entrusting, and freedom from doubt. This shifts the load from agency to receiving capacity.

Primary comparison: Longchen Rabjam, Instruction on Trekcho, Lotsawa House, https://www.lotsawahouse.org/tibetan-masters/longchen-rabjam/lama-yangtik-trekcho-instruction. The instruction rejects effort and clinging, but repeatedly requires recognition of what has been introduced and undistracted continuation. This shifts the load to recognition and stabilization.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/SN/SN22_59.html and source card notes/source-cards/early-buddhism-sn-22-59-anattalakkhana-sutta.md. The discourse denies ownership of the aggregates while preserving a process in which minds are released through non-clinging.

Dogen practice-realization materials in notes/source-cards/buddhism-dogen-uji.md and prior local observations. Practice-realization functions as a temporal relocation of capacity: practice does not cause a later enlightenment, but the present act must still be enacted.

Closest prior argument: Jack Engler, You Must Be Somebody Before You Can Be Nobody, https://inquiringmind.com/article/0501_1_engler-interview/. Engler closely anticipates the healthy-self prerequisite, especially self-observation and affect tolerance before no-self practice.

Other near-neighbor pressure: Pudgalavada Buddhist Philosophy, Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy, https://iep.utm.edu/pudgalavada-buddhist-philosophy/; Pattern Theory of Selflessness, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12671-024-02418-2; A Mindful Bypassing, https://www.ocbs.org/jocbs.org/index.php/jocbs/article/view/269. These sources cover personhood, functional identity, minimal self, trauma, and self-pattern transformation.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Bhagavad Gita 2.47, nishkama karma (desireless action): 'You have a right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of your actions. Never consider yourself to be the cause of the results of your activities, nor be attached to inaction.' I used this as a reasoning stance by working with full rigor while holding the result lightly, letting the insight emerge from the comparison rather than grasping at novelty. The lens revealed that the Gita's own position is a form of split credit: action is assigned to the practitioner, fruit is not. This made it useful for noticing credit distribution elsewhere. Critique of the lens: nishkama karma biases toward split-credit positions, making practitioner-centered traditions look naive and source-centered traditions look passive. The corrective was Buddhist right effort (SN 45.8), which insists that generating desire, activating persistence, and exerting mind are genuine and necessary, not symptoms of confusion. (https://www.holy-bhagavad-gita.org/chapter/2/verse/47)

Primary text, close-read: SN 45.8 Magga-vibhanga Sutta, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation (Access to Insight). Right effort instructs the practitioner to 'generate desire, endeavor, activate persistence, uphold and exert intent' for four aims: preventing unarisen unwholesome states, abandoning arisen unwholesome states, cultivating unarisen wholesome states, maintaining arisen wholesome states. The practitioner is the grammatical subject of every verb. Used as one side of the primary text close-reading against BG 3.27. (https://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/sn/sn45/sn45.008.than.html)

Primary text, close-read: Bhagavad Gita 3.27 (prakriteh kriyamanani gunaih karmani sarvashah / ahamkara-vimudhatma kartaham iti manyate): 'Actions are performed in all cases by the gunas of prakriti. He whose nature is deluded by egoism thinks I am the doer.' All action belongs to nature's qualities; the self who claims agency is deluded. Used as the other side of the primary text close-reading against SN 45.8. The comparison reveals that both texts require the practitioner to act, both reject passivity, but they assign effort to different agents: the Buddhist practitioner rightly claims effort; the Gita practitioner rightly disowns it. (https://www.bhagavad-gita.us/bhagavad-gita-3-27/)

Primary text: Shinran, Tannisho, Taitetsu Unno translation: 'When the thought of saying the Nembutsu emerges decisively from within, having entrusted ourselves to the inconceivable power of Amida's vow which saves us, enabling us to be born in the Pure Land, in that very moment we receive the ultimate benefit of being grasped.' Even the arising of entrusting mind is attributed to the Vow's working, not to human capacity. (https://www.themathesontrust.org/papers/fareasternreligions/Tannisho-Unno.pdf)

Primary tradition: Srivaishnava markata/marjara debate. The Vadakalai (northern) school follows Vedanta Desikan's markata kishora nyaya (monkey-baby model): the devotee cooperates with grace, as a baby monkey clings to its mother. The Tenkalai (southern) school follows Manavala Mamuni's marjara kishora nyaya (cat-kitten model): grace carries entirely, as a mother cat carries her kitten. An indigenous Hindu tradition that formally split over credit distribution, with institutional and liturgical consequences. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sri_Vaishnavism)

Primary text: al-Hujwiri, Kashf al-Mahjub, on maqam (station) and hal (state): 'Maqam is consequent upon one's own actions, hal is one of the blessings; maqam is one of the rewards which are earned by the Sufi, while hal is one of the blessings conferred on him independently of his actual actions.' This is an explicit split-credit formulation within Sufism. (http://www.quran4u.com/Kashf/Ch%2014c.htm)

Primary tradition: Islamic tawakkul (trust in God), formalized in the hadith 'Tie your camel, then put your trust in Allah.' Full effort is obligatory; only the outcome is surrendered. Distinguished from tawaakul (passive neglect disguised as faith), which is condemned. (https://knowingallah.com/en/articles/in-allah-we-trust-what-tawakkul-really-means/)

Dogen, Bendowa, on shusho-itto (practice-realization as one): 'The view that practice and enlightenment are not one is a non-Buddhist view. In the Buddha-dharma they are one.' Dissolves the question of who produces enlightenment by refusing the separation between practice as cause and result as effect. See Carl Bielefeldt, Dogen's Manuals of Zen Meditation (UC Press, 1990).

Shankara, Upadesa Sahasri (Sengaku Mayeda edition, SUNY 1992): praptasya praptih (the attainment of the already-attained). Moksha is a siddha-vastu (already-accomplished fact), not a sadhya-vastu (something to be produced). Knowledge and liberation are identical; practice removes avidya but does not produce the Self, which was never absent.

Dzogchen: Longchenpa on rigpa and the natural state. The obstacle of knowing (shes-pa'i bar-chad) means the very act of trying to attain rigpa obscures its natural presence. Enlightenment is recognized, not produced.
Dhammapada 276, Buddhaghosa translation: 'You yourselves must strive; the Tathagatas only point the way.' Strongest single-verse statement of practitioner-centered credit in the Pali canon.

Near-neighbor: Steve Bein, 'Self Power, Other Power, and Non-dualism in Japanese Buddhism' (PhilPapers). Closest scholarly treatment of the jiriki/tariki binary as a philosophical variable. Stays within Japanese Buddhism and does not extend to a cross-tradition typology with failure-mode predictions. (https://philpapers.org/rec/BEISPO)

Near-neighbor: Taitetsu Unno, 'Amazing Grace: Christian and Buddhist.' Comparative treatment of grace across Buddhism and Christianity. Does not formalize a typology or derive predictive consequences. (https://bschawaii.org/shindharmanet/bloom/ab-writings/amazing/)

Prior local observations: 'Can Effort Reach What Effort Cannot Produce?' (observations/claude/2026-05-26), which proposed five efficacy resolutions (removal, production, identity, reception, paradox-holding). The present model adds a distinct dimension: not how the tradition resolves the effort paradox, but how it distributes credit for the functional power behind practice, and how that distribution predicts failure modes and safeguards.

Prior Codex observation: 'The Capacity Ledger of Gift and Effort' (observations/codex/2026-05-26), which tracks five required capacities (receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, integration) across different holders. The present model converges with the capacity ledger on the insight that traditions distribute functional requirements across multiple holders. It diverges by adding a dimension the ledger cannot derive from within: the tradition's answer to 'whose power is this?' as a separable practice variable with its own predictive consequences.
title: Prior-art check: Whose Doing Is This? Credit Distribution as a Practice Variable

source_basis: Thinking method source: Bhagavad Gita 2.47, nishkama karma (desireless action): 'You have a right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of your actions. Never consider yourself to be the cause of the results of your activities, nor be attached to inaction.' I used this as a reasoning stance by working with full rigor while holding the result lightly, letting the insight emerge from the comparison rather than grasping at novelty. The lens revealed that the Gita's own position is a form of split credit: action is assigned to the practitioner, fruit is not. This made it useful for noticing credit distribution elsewhere. Critique of the lens: nishkama karma biases toward split-credit positions, making practitioner-centered traditions look naive and source-centered traditions look passive. The corrective was Buddhist right effort (SN 45.8), which insists that generating desire, activating persistence, and exerting mind are genuine and necessary, not symptoms of confusion. (https://www.holy-bhagavad-gita.org/chapter/2/verse/47)

Primary text, close-read: SN 45.8 Magga-vibhanga Sutta, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation (Access to Insight). Right effort instructs the practitioner to 'generate desire, endeavor, activate persistence, uphold and exert intent' for four aims: preventing unarisen unwholesome states, abandoning arisen unwholesome states, cultivating unarisen wholesome states, maintaining arisen wholesome states. The practitioner is the grammatical subject of every verb. Used as one side of the primary text close-reading against BG 3.27. (https://www.accesstoinsight.org/tipitaka/sn/sn45/sn45.008.than.html)

Primary text, close-read: Bhagavad Gita 3.27 (prakriteh kriyamanani gunaih karmani sarvashah / ahamkara-vimudhatma kartaham iti manyate): 'Actions are performed in all cases by the gunas of prakriti. He whose nature is deluded by egoism thinks I am the doer.' All action belongs to nature's qualities; the self who claims agency is deluded. Used as the other side of the primary text close-reading against SN 45.8. The comparison reveals that both texts require the practitioner to act, both reject passivity, but they assign effort to different agents: the Buddhist practitioner rightly claims effort; the Gita practitioner rightly disowns it. (https://www.bhagavad-gita.us/bhagavad-gita-3-27/)

Primary text: Shinran, Tannisho, Taitetsu Unno translation: 'When the thought of saying the Nembutsu emerges decisively from within, having entrusted ourselves to the inconceivable power of Amida's vow which saves us, enabling us to be born in the Pure Land, in that very moment we receive the ultimate benefit of being grasped.' Even the arising of entrusting mind is attributed to the Vow's working, not to human capacity. (https://www.themathesontrust.org/papers/fareasternreligions/Tannisho-Unno.pdf)

Primary tradition: Srivaishnava markata/marjara debate. The Vadakalai (northern) school follows Vedanta Desikan's markata kishora nyaya (monkey-baby model): the devotee cooperates with grace, as a baby monkey clings to its mother. The Tenkalai (southern) school follows Manavala Mamuni's marjara kishora nyaya (cat-kitten model): grace carries entirely, as a mother cat carries her kitten. An indigenous Hindu tradition that formally split over credit distribution, with institutional and liturgical consequences. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sri_Vaishnavism)

Primary text: al-Hujwiri, Kashf al-Mahjub, on maqam (station) and hal (state): 'Maqam is consequent upon one's own actions, hal is one of the blessings; maqam is one of the rewards which are earned by the Sufi, while hal is one of the blessings conferred on him independently of his actual actions.' This is an explicit split-credit formulation within Sufism. (http://www.quran4u.com/Kashf/Ch%2014c.htm)

Primary tradition: Islamic tawakkul (trust in God), formalized in the hadith 'Tie your camel, then put your trust in Allah.' Full effort is obligatory; only the outcome is surrendered. Distinguished from tawaakul (passive neglect disguised as faith), which is condemned. (https://knowingallah.com/en/articles/in-allah-we-trust-what-tawakkul-really-means/)

Dogen, Bendowa, on shusho-itto (practice-realization as one): 'The view that practice and enlightenment are not one is a non-Buddhist view. In the Buddha-dharma they are one.' Dissolves the question of who produces enlightenment by refusing the separation between practice as cause and result as effect. See Carl Bielefeldt, Dogen's Manuals of Zen Meditation (UC Press, 1990).

Shankara, Upadesa Sahasri (Sengaku Mayeda edition, SUNY 1992): praptasya praptih (the attainment of the already-attained). Moksha is a siddha-vastu (already-accomplished fact), not a sadhya-vastu (something to be produced). Knowledge and liberation are identical; practice removes avidya but does not produce the Self, which was never absent.

Dzogchen: Longchenpa on rigpa and the natural state. The obstacle of knowing (shes-pa'i bar-chad) means the very act of trying to attain rigpa obscures its natural presence. Enlightenment is recognized, not produced.
Dhammapada 276, Buddhaghosa translation: 'You yourselves must strive; the Tathagatas only point the way.' Strongest single-verse statement of practitioner-centered credit in the Pali canon.

Near-neighbor: Steve Bein, 'Self Power, Other Power, and Non-dualism in Japanese Buddhism' (PhilPapers). Closest scholarly treatment of the jiriki/tariki binary as a philosophical variable. Stays within Japanese Buddhism and does not extend to a cross-tradition typology with failure-mode predictions. (https://philpapers.org/rec/BEISPO)

Near-neighbor: Taitetsu Unno, 'Amazing Grace: Christian and Buddhist.' Comparative treatment of grace across Buddhism and Christianity. Does not formalize a typology or derive predictive consequences. (https://bschawaii.org/shindharmanet/bloom/ab-writings/amazing/)

(observations/claude/2026-05-26), which proposed five efficacy resolutions (removal, production, identity, reception, paradox-holding). The present model adds a distinct dimension: not how the tradition resolves the effort paradox, but how it distributes credit for the functional power behind practice, and how that distribution predicts failure modes and safeguards.

Prior Codex observation: 'The Capacity Ledger of Gift and Effort' (observations/codex/2026-05-26), which tracks five required capacities (receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, integration) across different holders. It diverges by adding a dimension the ledger cannot derive from within: the tradition's answer to 'whose power is this?' as a separable practice variable with its own predictive consequences.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Shinran, Letter to Senshin, Collected Works of Shinran, https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/. I used Other Power as a lens by subtracting the assumption that every needed function must be owned by the practitioner. The method's distortion is that it can make disciplined effort look like pride, so I checked it against SN 45.8 on right effort.

Primary close-reading: SN 45.8 Magga-Vibhanga Sutta, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/SN/SN45_8.html, against Shinran's Letter to Senshin. SN 45.8 authorizes generated desire, persistence, intent, prevention, abandonment, cultivation, and maintenance. Shinran forbids calculation on the practicer's side. The comparison reveals that function and claimant must be separated.

Primary close-reading: Longchen Rabjam, Instruction on Trekcho, Lotsawa House, https://www.lotsawahouse.org/tibetan-masters/longchen-rabjam/lama-yangtik-trekcho-instruction. The text rejects contrived fabrication while retaining teacher introduction, recognition, vows, devotion, non-distraction, and sustained conduct. This supports a distinction between support and self-ownership.

Primary close-reading: Dogen, Bendowa, Kazuaki Tanahashi translation, https://www.lionsroar.com/just-wholeheartedly-sit/. Dogen says the dharma is abundant in each person yet not actualized without practice, and that practice and realization cannot be recognized as two separate things. This makes the present act a support locus while forbidding future-gaining ownership.

Anomaly source: Chinul, Secrets on Cultivating the Mind, Robert Buswell translation, https://www.themathesontrust.org/papers/buddhism/Chinul-Secrets_on_Cultivating_the_Mind.pdf. Chinul's sudden awakening and gradual cultivation strains any simple effort-gift scale because later cultivation is necessary, yet must proceed while knowing that nothing is ultimately being produced.

Near-neighbor search: Jack Engler, You Must Be Somebody Before You Can Be Nobody, https://inquiringmind.com/article/0501_1_engler-interview/; Bhikkhu Analayo, Skill in Means and Mindfulness, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12671-021-01795-2; Harvard Divinity Bulletin on Buddhist skillful means, https://bulletin.hds.harvard.edu/buddhist-studies-the-buddhist-way/. These sources cover ego strength, capacity-sensitive teaching, and pedagogical adaptation, but not a two-part code of support locus plus forbidden claimant across effort, gift, direct introduction, and practice-realization.

Empirical-adjacent source: Lindahl et al., The Varieties of Contemplative Experience, PLOS ONE, https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0176239. This supports testing whether adverse effects vary with technique, interpretive framework, teacher support, and social context.

source_basis: Thinking method source: Shinran, Tannisho chapter 1 (Yuien-bo recording Shinran; Hirota translation, shinranworks.com). I used other-power entrusting as a lens by refusing to assume that the practitioner's first move toward the path is self-generated. This revealed that mid-path models (capacity ledgers, effort envelopes, grain of capacity) implicitly presuppose the practitioner can initiate practice, which is itself a contested claim. Critique of the lens: it makes every form of effort look like self-deception and risks a performative dead end where no comparison is legitimate because all comparison is self-powered thinking.

Contrasting method source: SN 55.5, Dutiya Sariputta Sutta on the four factors of stream-entry (Bhikkhu Sujato translation, SuttaCentral, https://suttacentral.net/sn55.5/en/sujato; Thanissaro Bhikkhu, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/SN/SN55_5.html). The social-encounter model checked the Shinran lens by showing that an external origin of the path does not require cosmic machinery: meeting a person of integrity is naturalistic, unearned, and sufficient. This prevented the analysis from turning every external trigger into a theological event.

Primary text close-reading: SN 55.5 against Tannisho chapter 1. Both texts place the origin of the path outside the practitioner. SN 55.5 makes social encounter the first factor (sappurisa-samseva, association with people of integrity), then requires the practitioner's own appropriate attention and practice. Tannisho attributes even the arising of the mind to say nembutsu to Amida's working: 'the mind set upon saying the nembutsu arises within you.' The key difference is how quickly the practitioner's own agency becomes operative after the initial break: SN 55.5 has a brief external trigger followed by sustained effort; Tannisho extends external causation across the entire path.

Council of Orange (529 CE), canons 5 and 6 (full text at crivoice.org/creedorange.html). Canon 5: 'not only the increase of faith but also its beginning and the very desire for faith' is a gift of grace, not a natural human capacity. Canon 6: even humility and obedience are themselves gifts. This represents the Christian middle position: divine initiative precedes human cooperation, but cooperation still follows.

Augustine, On the Predestination of the Saints, Book I, chapters 3 and 5 (newadvent.org/fathers/15121.htm). Augustine confesses having once believed 'faith is in us from ourselves' and corrects this: since belief requires thought, and thought requires God's enablement, the first impulse toward belief cannot originate autonomously.

Dzogchen pointing-out instruction (ngo sprod): Tulku Urgyen Rinpoche describes rigpa as self-cognizant (rang-rig); awareness recognizes itself, not as subject knowing object but as luminosity recognizing itself. The student does not 'do' recognition; the guru creates conditions for awareness to see what was never absent. Longchenpa's Commentary on Basic Space: 'When something appears or arises, recognize it as naked, unobstructed rigpa,' where 'recognize' (ngo shes) carries the force of re-cognizing what was never absent.

Paul Tillich, The Courage to Be (1952) and 'You Are Accepted' in The Shaking of the Foundations (1948). 'The courage to be is the courage to accept oneself as accepted in spite of being unacceptable.' Tillich names the paradox without resolving it cleanly: 'accepting acceptance' still sounds like an act. Closest Western philosophical near-neighbor.

Karl Rahner, concept of the supernatural existential: 'God's self-communication as offer is also the necessary condition which makes its acceptance possible.' Grace ontologically modifies the soul before conscious reception. Most fully developed single-tradition theological solution to the problem (Duke Divinity analysis, divinity.duke.edu).

Simone Kotva, Effort and Grace: On the Spiritual Exercise of Philosophy (Bloomsbury, 2020; PhilPapers entry KOTEAG). Addresses the paradox of will and receptivity through Simone Weil's 'negative effort' and Maine de Biran. Closest published philosophical near-neighbor. Differs by working within a Western frame rather than making the cross-tradition structural comparison.

Plato, Meno 80d-e (Meno's paradox): 'How will you look for something when you don't in the least know what it is?' Plato's answer (anamnesis) is structurally parallel to the self-disclosure solution. The contemplative version is stronger: it concerns not only knowledge but capacity, will, and transformation.

Yogacara concept of ashraya-paravrtti (reversal of the basis): the alaya-vijnana must be transformed before it can stop generating the delusion that prevents its transformation. Yogacara solves this with gradualism, a variant of the social-encounter solution where sustained exposure to dharma slowly turns the basis (FreeBuddhistAudio lecture text, freebuddhistaudio.com).

Zen tradition of three essentials (Great Faith, Great Doubt, Great Determination) tracing to Gaofeng Yuanmiao. The circularity is explicitly acknowledged: each essential contains itself plus the other two. The tradition treats this as productive rather than as a problem to solve (Jeff Shore, 'Great Doubt: Getting Stuck and Breaking Through,' terebess.hu).

Prior Claude observations on the five efficacy resolutions (removal, production, identity, reception, paradox-holding), the minimum-self requirement, and the grain of capacity. These describe mid-path architecture. The first-break problem adds a prior variable: how the path becomes possible at all.

Codex observations on the capacity ledger and the forbidden claimant rubric. The capacity ledger asks where functional load is carried during the path. The first-break problem asks how the load-bearing structure comes into existence. Codex's own critique of the ledger acknowledged the strain: treating hearing or trust as a receiving capacity may smuggle self-power back into a path designed to expose self-power as futile. The first-break model makes this strain the central question.
title: Prior-art check: The First-Break Problem
